speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Stella Faitek on March the 6th, 1997. Uh, just, uh, as far as the-- the Communist youth organization, uh, do you remember-- did they try to indoctrinate you, to teach you to think in a certain way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember, one time, we came to the meeting, and the instructor was a young man. And he said that, here, in, uh, in Poland, in the factories, it's, uh-- they would-- where they make sweaters. They crippled the people when they worked by the machine. When they are crippled, they are more producing the work. And I said, in God-- in God's name, how can they do it to human beings? They did lie all kind of things. That in Russia, it's beautiful. There is no poor people. Everybody is the same. Lies and lies all the time. But when I came to Russia, I saw the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the outbreak of the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: September, '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Everybody was running to stores to buy, to-- to have food for the house. Everybody was talking that the war broke out, but we will win. Polish army is strong. We will win.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the three-- the first day, saw how trag-- tragic it is. It was very-- people were scared. They start to run. The youth went out from Warsaw in the hundreds. I went out in a group, 12 people, with mine husband, his brother, and his wife, and friends. Was this from the Communist group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: --from sports, from sport club. And we wal-- walked two weeks to the Russian border. And there, the airplanes were flying-- the Russian airplanes-- throwing leaflets. The Russian Army is coming to free you from capitalism. So we're staying in lines-- two lines-- waiting for the Russians, and they came in. Uh, when they came in, they announced that we will go to the jail to break the jail and to let the people out. There are communists arrested. So we went there. We freed-- we broke the-- the jail. The people came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was it? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Rovno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rovno-- it was the border, by the Russian border. And uh, then, I remembered one man saw me there. And he said, you know, I will report you. You will get whatever you need. It was time of war. It was very bad. We didn't have food. But there was a-- a lady which took me to her house. And I was there. And we were making parties. And they brought beer and food. Everything we had there, until they came there. And I got a job. I worked in a magazine, like [? here, MP. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Rovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Rovno. Then they announced that we can go wherever we want to go. And I want to go-- we decided to go to Pinsk when we went out from Poland, from Warsaw. We decided to walk to Pinsk, but we were lost in the time of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Pinsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pinsk-- it is close to the Russian border. And in Pinsk was a woman which was working by mine husband. She was a working-- she was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband-- your husband was a tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had a store? He had a tailor shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: A tailor shop in Warsaw. And she was working with us. So we decided to go there. When we came there-- until we came there, we were lost, because the war-- we-- I remember that I was walking over dead bodies. Uh, lots of people were killed there. They bombarded. They were shooting-- unbelievable. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was from the-- the-- the Russians who killed them? Or the Germans who killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. And then, we were lost. And I was in Rovno. And the other group was already in Pinsk, when we-- when I was free. And the man which, uh, helped me with everything, I decided to go to Pinsk to look for them. And we went there. There, we-- he organized a shop-- a tailor shop. We worked, until I went from there. I went to Bialystok to look for a job. I got there a job, and I worked there. And I took mine brother from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: From?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the group-- they were in Pinsk, and I was in Rovno. From Rovno, I went to Pinsk to look for them. So when I found them, I decided to go to Bialystok, which is a bigger city. There, I got a job. And I-- from there, I took mine husband and mine brothers. They were with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, as far as Hitler and the Nazis, you had heard about them when you were working in the-- the orphanage as a social worker. Uh, what were your feelings about them? What-- what had you-- what did you hear? And what did you think about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know-- I know that they have Dachau in Germany-- a jail. But we didn't know-- we didn't believe that they can do things which they did. They are human-- we thought that they are human people the-- who are-- they are against the-- for the country, but not against people. But when we had start to listen to it, we couldn't believe. And I even want to go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- because-- you heard about the-- the anti-Semitism, you heard about the Nazi anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was when are you-- it was late, when I was already in, uh, Bialystok, we heard it. But still, we couldn't believe in the things which happened later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had-- did you hear, though-- in the 1930s, did you hear about anti-Semitism in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I heard it, and I remember they used to send out Polish Jews from Germany to Poland. And I remember a uncle came from Germany. We stayed with mine uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rich uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he was stay-- staying there. And I remember, in papers were written, and lots of Polish Jews were chasing out from Germany. And one-- a few Jew-- Jewish men came with German woman-- wives. They came with them. The anti-Semitism-- I mean, anti-Semitism was very big in Germany. We knew about it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were in Bialystok, did you hear what was happening to the Jews under German control? Under-- the Polish Jews under German control?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Bialystok. In Bialystok, we didn't hear so much. But when we were deep in Russia, we heard in the radio. And in the paper, we can read. But otherwise, we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you returned to Warsaw. From Bialystok, you went back to Warsaw--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to try to bring the two children from the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children were dead at that time. The children-- I brought them 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I located them in a orphanage in Bialystok. They were young children.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Bialystok, they were to sent deep to Russia. I got one letter from them that they are OK, that they are thankful to me that they are alive. And the younger one, he was thrown away child. He said, you are my mother, and I will never forget you. I know that, if they would be alive, they would look for me. I look for them through Red Cross, all kind of organization. They probably died. It was hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names? Kubish Rodkin. The other is Haskelic. I don't remember his last name.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And so, when you were-- [SIGHS] when you were go-- go-- on the way to-- to Rovno, and ultimately to Pinsk, you took them with you in the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I locate them in a orphanage in Bialystok. I couldn't take them with me. I didn't have the opportunity to give them anything. I located them in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But-- but they went-- they went with you originally when you left Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I took them to Bialystok. In Bialystok, I located them in a orphanage. I cannot take them with me. They are young, and I couldn't take care of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once you were in Bialystok, you never-- in other words, in the Russian zone of occupation, you never tried to go back to Warsaw from the Russian zone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In Bialystok, was a rail station. Who wants to go deep to Russia? Or who wants to go back to Poland? I registered to go back to Poland, because I was disgusted. I couldn't believe them. I hated them. And I registered to go back to Poland. I knew that's bad. But when you are disgusted with the things-- like, I believed in them. Mine whole life was broken because of this. I was disappointed, and disappointment can bring you to anything. And I decided to go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the disappointment came because-- why? Why? Why were you so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I saw the lies which is going on there. I was-- the-- uh, long story-- I was working in magazine. Since I was a communist, I got a privilege to get a good job. And at that time, they arrested people, uh, young men, which are without work. They arrested them. In between them was my brother. And a group saw my brother arrested. They came to the store where I was working, and they told me that they saw your brother arrested. So I ran to the police to free him, to find out where he is. And then, [RUSSIAN] said to me, the director said to me, well, if we want somebody to find, we can find him. I said, they are working. They are not, uh, against the communism, against your country. We will not free him. He will go to jail. And since I was so sure with myself, and I said, no, I will-- I will not let you arrest him, to send him anywhere. And he started to knock on the table. You get out from here, because I will arrest you. I said, I am not afraid. You can arrest me. And [SIGHS] he called two policeman. They arrested me. They sent me to a cell. I was there. And I-- after an hour, he called me back. And he said, you go home, and I will not let your brother free. I said, I will not go home. I want to be here in jail. He was afraid, probably, because he used to, uh, make orders, what to bring in to his office, because they cannot take out from the store, because it's not-- not legal to take out from the store what they want. So I used to bring it to him. And I said, I will not go out from here, until you will not free my brother. And he started to knock at the table. You go out. I said, no, I will not go out. I am going, he said, home. I'm going to eat lunch. I said, I will wait here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came back. He didn't free him. And I went home. And my brother was sent somewhere. I didn't know where he was sent. So mine disappointment was very strong. So I came back. What-- what kind of work did you do on the magazine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saleslady. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sell--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I was the manager. I received everything, and I gave out to the di-- to dining room all the material which they need for cooking lunches. It was in magazine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was responsible for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the magazine was a-- was a-- that was a kitchen. It was a kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: The pred-- By-- by the magazine was the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to the kitchen, I gave out the produce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except if we had bread, sugar--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. The magazine was a storehouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a ware-- storehouse. OK. Sure. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I gave out, every day, people bread, sugar, whatever I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and this was in Rovno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ro-- no. This was in, uh, Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Bialystok. So you were in Bialystok from 1939, un-- for how long?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the Germans attacked the Russians-- was '49?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they attacked Russian, they took us out from Bialystok deep to Russia. But through the concentration camp also, but it's not like in Germany. We were in the woods-- working in the woods. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they tell you that they were taking you, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was registered to go home. I went-- was considered the [RUSSIAN]-- enemy of Russian-- communism. So that's why we were in concentration camp. Instead sending, they didn't send anybody back to Poland. The people which registered to go back to-- go to Russia, they went free, to Russian's count-- Russian towns, wherever they want to go. And us, they sent to the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, when Germany invaded Russia, they-- they-- the communists-- the local communists there put you in Bialystok-- put you on a train, you and your husband. And they sent you where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vologda-- [RUSSIAN]. It's not far from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Leningrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the-- to the east?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Farther away from the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by this time, you were disillusioned with communism? Or you still felt that there was hope under the communists?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't believe in mem-- in them. But now, the war goes on. Nobody knows what will be part or what will happen. So we were there working.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Vologda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of-- was it slave labor? Or paid labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slave labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slave labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were cutting trees-- big trees. I never knew the work. And you have to make as much that you can get the money to buy the soup, which they make. It was very hard work. But later, I got a job. In beginning, I worked in the woods. But later, I got the job in the magazine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kind of a-- a storehouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out, when the Germans attacked the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband, in Vologda, was working, also cutting trees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Vologda?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942-- beginning. And I was pregnant at that time. Leon was born April 26, '42. So probably, when I was the five-- fifth, sixth, month pregnant, we went free from, uh, Birsk-- from-- uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Vologda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vologda to Birsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they sent you-- they shipped you to- to Birsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Birsk, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea where Birsk was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you were being sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a-- the slightest idea where it is. But it was one man was with us, which was in the World War I, and he remembered that there is bread-- lots of bread, and he decided to go there. So we decided to go with him. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so you weren't forced to go to Birsk, you went of your free-- of your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I could go wherever I want to, but not to the first, uh, states. This is B state. They're not the best states, though. They're the little states. So I didn't know where to go. But the man which was with us, he said that this is a good place. I was never hungry there in time of-- of World War I. So we went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So you-- you could have gone, maybe, to Kazakhstan, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A lot of people went there, where it's warm, where it's-- nobody knew which-- which city is better. But we went there. But everybody was in the states where it is B, not A.

speaker: interviewer
text: A would be more like Moscow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moscow, Leningrad-- no. To this, you cannot get a ride.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't, because there was already a siege. And then, what happened when you arrived at Birsk? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived at Birsk, I came to Ufa. From Ufa, we are waiting to go to Birsk. And the only way to go to Birsk is by boat, not taxis, not trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: From- from Ufa?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Ufa to Birsk. We were sitting there and waiting for a, uh, for a ship, for a boat. And, uh, I was pregnant. And I was sick with, uh-- I had a cold. And all of a sudden, I see a woman came toward my bench where I was sitting with a big suitcase-- written, Leningrad, Moscow, Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she sat next to me, and she asked me, where are you going? I said, I am going to Birsk. Birsk? I am going to Birsk too. My mother lives in Birsk. Why do you speak so-- not loud? And I said, I am sick-- bronchitis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she took me to a place where she cleaned me. I-- I was dirty. She cleaned me. She gave me something to-- another-- another dress. And she disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I am sitting on the bench. All of a sudden, she comes back. She said, oh, we are going with the first class to Birsk. I said to my husband, what is it? A dream? We came in the first class-- white tablecloths, silverware. And she opened the suitcase, said, Stella, take what you want. I said, I don't want anything. I don't need anything. In the radio, they said, now, we'll play Borisovna Rivchun on D flat. He said, oh, oh, this is mine brother. I said, that's a dream. What is it? She opened the suitcase. There is a fiddle, a Stradivarius. She's the first fiddler in Moscow theater. And she said, Stella, you will go with me. And I was very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went there in the middle of the night. She got a horse and a buggy. She said, your suitcase, put on the buggy. And you and I, with Israel, will walk. It was a hill to walk there. The horse--

speaker: interviewer
text: And Israel, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the horse was skinny-- hungry. I thought that the horse will die before everything. We came up. It was in the middle of the night. She knocks at the window. Momichka, we have guests. A grey lady with beautiful eyes came out with a smile. Yeah, come on. You're welcome. You're welcome. And she said, you take a shower. I'll give you the bed. You will sleep there. And I said, no. We will sleep on the floor. We will not get to the bed. And they took marvelous care of us. It was just a dream. Who would think about a thing like this. You are going, not knowing where you are going, where you will stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, when I was there, the people which came there before us, they found mine brother on the way. Mine brother was running from one wagon to the other, asks, Stella? You know Stella, my sister, Stella? I was known, famous, in Poland. He thought that I am-- I'm famous-- famous in Russia too. You were famous because of sport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was Joseph? Or Samuel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam. And the-- he finally got the-- the train, which people were with me, they said, oh yes, Stella will come to Birsk. She's-- she will be here. He came there, and he had the laryngitis. He was sick, in hospital. When I came there a few days later, I found out that my brother is there. And I want to go to the hospital. He was in hospital. And the Emma, the lady which took us to Birsk, said, no, you will not go. I will go with your husband. We will find out everything. You are sick. You will be well. You will go there. They went to him. They saw him. And when he came out of the hospital, they got a apartment for us. And, uh, then, they got, uh, work for mine husband. And we were on their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name-- the name of this-- of the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emma Borisovna Rivchun. She was the first fiddler in Moscow theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was her-- her-- Rivchun? R--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rivchun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rivchun. And so you-- during the war then, for the rest of the war, you stayed in Birsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: From 1942, until 1940--

speaker: interviewer
text: Until 1946.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you hu-- what kind of work was your husband doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. In-- in Birsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I freed him from the woods, because he was sick. He had the ulcer. I was working, he wasn't working. And from there, we went back to Poland. Now, we were corresponding to-- with, uh, Emma Rivchun. They went back to Moscow. They were living in Moscow. And she wrote letters to us. We were registered back to Poland. There-- 1946- 45 was a agreement-- the Polish government with the Russian government-- to let the Polish people go out from Russia to Poland back. And we decided to go back to Poland. And I wrote to them a letter. And she writes it's-- not to go back to Poland. It's not good to go to Poland, because it's anti-Semitism. That she has a job for mine husband to sew for Stalin in a apartment in Moscow. Only a person like she could get things. And we decided to go back to Poland, because I still was dreaming, maybe my father's alive, maybe my sister's alive. We didn't know that everybody is destroyed. And we went-- I wrote to her, I thank you very much for it, but we decided to go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during the-- the years in Birsk, then, how-- how did you support yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Birsk, I had the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the store, in the magazine.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this-- in this kind of a warehouse or store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your hus--

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and your husband was not--

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't doing-- yeah, he had-- they told him to be a fireman. If a fire will be, to run to the office, this was his job. But he had ulcer, so he didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon was born in 1942. It was [INAUDIBLE]. It was very hard-- no food. And-- but somehow, we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you discover that, uh, that Leon was a hemophiliac?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a year old. He fell, and he bite his tongue. And he was bleeding. I put him to bed. It was daytime. In the morning, the whole bed was in blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the doctor. And the doctor said, you know what? He has a cut. I will sew it. I said, Doctor, if you sew it, you'll make holes. It will bleed more. He said, it's a good idea. I'll make a test, it's not hemophilia. He made the test, and he told me it's a hemophilia. Then I found out. But it was a blessing from all this-- all sides. That you were able to-- to find out without any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that-- no, I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh. It's a irony.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, it's the blessing from all sides-- the war, and mine husband sick with the ulcers. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. I understand.
